wvLvCuer8uU: This 1918 Marshall & Wendell arrived to us as a former player piano, its old pumping mechanism long since removed, in need of a full restoration inside and out. We refinished the case with a replica of its original decal, kept and refinished its original bench, replated the pedals, and rebuilt the action end to end with new strings, tuning pins, dampers, and hammers, all set beneath a freshly repainted plate. Restored to its full power and voice, it now graces a bed and breakfast in Meredith, New Hampshire.
pVUaEyI-gSg: A client recalled this as the piano he grew up playing as a boy, so when it came in for a complete rebuild, he chose a finish to match his memory of its original color. We rebuilt the entire instrument, including a new soundboard and bridge caps and a freshly refinished plate, restored its pneumatic player mechanism to full working order, and refinished the case throughout. It's now back in a home in Orem, Utah, carrying that childhood memory forward.
a2CVwblqOe0: Nearly a century ago, this upright served as an early jukebox of sorts — a coin dropped in its slot would set the mechanism playing behind a small locking window in a saloon or billiard hall. It arrived in rough shape, and we rebuilt it completely with new strings, hammers, and dampers, a freshly gold-painted plate, a replica decal, and a custom brass-electroplated 3D-printed piece designed to fill the gap left by its long-missing lock. Restored to a rich, full tone, it now lives in Bountiful, Utah.
1bEk7S1AMnU: This 1915 upright, originally built in New York, came to us needing extensive bridge work, including a full rebuild of the treble bridge the length of the piano, along with a complete top-to-bottom restoration. The case had never carried a decal, so at the owners' request we added one for the first time, alongside new keytops, hammers, strings, and a plate finished in fresh automotive paint. Rebuilt for a big, warm, rich voice, it's now settled into a home in Draper, Utah.
bUDy7kTkpZA: This high-end upright from the player piano era underwent a rare four-part restoration: the instrument, its pneumatic player mechanism, the cosmetics, and its original ivory keytops. We salvaged what could be saved of the antique ivory and filled in the rest from our own reclaimed stock, refinished the plate in automotive paint, and rebuilt the action with new tuning pins, strings, dampers, and hammers alongside a full rebuild of the player's airtight bellows system. A family heirloom brought back to its original grandeur, it now plays on in Orem, Utah.
1ZZzzQHGYN4: A client who has owned this piano since he was a young man in the 1960s never much cared for its sound growing up, but he had it fully restored as a gift for his daughter. We refinished the case and veneer, re-covered the keys, added new hammers and triple bass dampers, replaced worn bushings and cloth throughout the action, and repaired separation in the soundboard where it had pulled loose from its ribs. When he came to try it afterward, he said it had never sounded so rich and full — a family piano now passed down to the next generation in Payson, Utah.
F6vSkXkrbSM: This 1929 grand came to us completely painted white, its case and internals both in rough shape after a century of use, and we gave it a full rebuild: new strings, dampers, hammers, shanks, and a replica decal, along with a pneumatic player system added at the owner's request. The original ivory keytops were kept and cleaned, with a few chipped pieces replaced from our own reclaimed ivory stock so the keyboard stayed largely original. It quickly became a favorite among our own technicians for its tone, and it's ready for decades more of music in Sandy, Utah.
NOFTljyDgXo: This 1933 player piano, built in New York, arrived needing everything: a full refinishing of its case in a lighter, original-style veneer, and a complete rebuild of the instrument inside, from new strings and tuning pins to duplicated bridge caps, a new bass bridge, and a freshly refinished soundboard. Its intricate pneumatic player mechanism, with an individual bellows for every note, was entirely rebuilt and made airtight again after a century of wear. Restored better than the day it was built, it now plays on in Las Vegas, Nevada.
8cGVjfS6tpc: This 1925 Mason & Hamlin grand -- a brand long favored by discerning pianists -- came to us in need of a full inside-out rebuild. Over roughly eight months we replaced the entire action with new hammers and hardware, refinished the soundboard and repaired its cracks, rebuilt the case in a deep automotive-grade finish, and carefully cleaned and polished the original ivory keys rather than replace them. The result plays and sounds like a brand-new instrument, now at home in Orem, Utah.
BOcwuBHU8qM: This 1895 Ivers & Pond upright is treasured as a family heirloom, chosen originally for its unusually tasteful, restrained ornamentation rather than the heavier carving common on pianos of its era. We gave it a full rebuild -- new hammers and hammer butts, fresh leather and cloth throughout the action, a refinished plate with hand-buffed original lettering, new bass strings sourced from a specialist in Germany, and rebuilt pedal trapwork. More than a century after it was built, it's ready to be played for generations to come in Pueblo West, Colorado.
RXxJpnD_NAc: This 1922 Baldwin grand belongs to a family with two young boys, who asked us to hold off on refinishing the case until the kids are a bit older and focus instead on a complete interior rebuild. We restored tension to the soundboard, refinished the plate, replaced the entire action -- hammers, dampers, shanks, and whippens -- torqued every tuning pin to proper spec, replaced the keys, and polished the original hardware and pedals. Now it produces a big, rich tone with impressively long sustain, waiting in Murray, Utah, for two future piano players to grow into it.
-GyreMXMHx8: This small English-made baby grand -- believed to be about a century old, though its serial number no longer confirms an exact year -- arrived sounding lackluster and left with a completely rebuilt interior, from new hammers, shanks, and dampers to a refinished plate in a rich automotive-grade paint. Its fallboard reveals a strikingly beautiful satin veneer that surprised even our own team, and once restrung and regulated it produced far more power and depth than a piano this size usually can. It's the second heirloom this family has entrusted to us, and now it's ready to carry the tradition forward in Saratoga Springs, Utah.
0Dlw-gquDIE: This 1922 Gulbransen player piano recreates a client's childhood memory of the Gulbransen her family once owned, tracked down out of state and brought in for a complete restoration of the case, action, and player mechanism alike. The original player ran on an electric vacuum motor, but at her request we converted it back to a foot-pumped system -- retrofitting a salvaged trunk from a retired piano and even machining a 3D-printed part to make it work, alongside a vintage pressure bar rescued from a defunct local piano factory. Freshly refinished, restrung, and re-hammered, it now fills a home in Coppell, Texas, with the same foot-powered music remembered from childhood.
94-dy4ykjFA: At 50 years old, this Baldwin grand was in good enough shape that we took a hybrid approach rather than a full rebuild -- installing new hammers, shanks, knuckles, flanges, dampers, and other felt and cloth components, while cleaning and retaining the original strings, tuning pins, plate, and soundboard, all of which had held up remarkably well. We also refinished the case in a smooth satin finish and voiced the tone down slightly from its naturally bright character to a more balanced sound. The piano was delivered essentially playing like new to its home in Cottonwood Heights, Utah.
BK_1IKaahHg: This compact 1974 Baldwin Acrosonic upright needed little beyond a fresh look -- we refinished the case in a custom tinted lacquer chosen from a set of color samples, polished the pedals, and gave it a full tuning. Baldwins of this smaller size have a knack for outperforming their footprint, with surprising projection and a touch response that belies their compact frame. Freshly finished and tuned, it now anchors a home in Lehi, Utah.
w1mulB0AZ40: Built in 1923, during what we consider the golden age of American piano making, this Steinway model O grand came to us for a complete rebuild -- new strings, tuning pins, dampers, and a full action, plus new bridge caps set atop the original bridge routs. We refinished the case to a rich satin walnut and cleaned up its original ivory keytops, replacing only a handful of pieces with vintage ivory from our own reserves, since new ivory hasn't been available since it was banned in 1971. Complete with its rare surviving matching bench, this Steinway is ready for its next home in Salt Lake City, Utah.
uVzt4M33wMw: This Henry F. Miller upright first passed through our workshop years ago as a lightly restored piano built on spec, and the family who eventually bought it lived with it for nearly a decade before their teenage son's love of playing inspired them to bring it back for a full rebuild. We installed new tuning pins, strings, hammers, and dampers, repainted the cast iron plate, and stabilized several soundboard cracks by re-shimming and retensioning the board rather than treating them as a serious flaw. A faint factory stamp found inside dates the piano to 1912, and it is now back home in Lehi, Utah, ready for another century of use.
3bJ9EoNWGLI: This 1935 Knabe Ampico B represents player-piano technology near its peak, built with expression and repeat controls remarkably advanced for an instrument made in the depths of the Great Depression. One of our technicians spent roughly 400 hours reviving the player mechanism alone, while the piano received new bass strings and tuning pins along with fresh hammers, shanks, knuckles, and flanges, with the rest of its original parts carefully refurbished rather than replaced to preserve its 1935 character. It now plays on in Baker, Montana, its ornate control panel and rewind-and-repeat features fully restored.
oEywVb3Sbls: Built in the late 1800s, this Schimmel & Nelson is a genuine oddity of a true upright grand: its plate is shaped like a grand piano's, with the strings struck down near the bottom of the case instead of up top, giving it a surprisingly big, grand-piano voice. It appears to be the only piano of its kind these makers ever produced, and it is easy to see why -- reaching the tuning pins means removing the legs and knee board, and its stubborn bass-to-treble break makes it notoriously difficult to voice and tune. After a full rebuild with new strings and hammers, this piano now lives in Ballard, Utah, keeping remarkably steady tune despite its unusual and demanding design.
Jp8BB252_yc: At not quite 60 years old, this Cable-Nelson upright was a good candidate for a thorough refurbishing rather than a ground-up restoration, so the focus was on reshaping and voicing the hammers, replacing worn felt and cloth, fitting new key bushings, and completing a full regulation to bring the action back to like-new feel. The case was refinished and given a new decal replicating the original, along with a fresh set of keys. This long-loved family piano now carries that legacy forward in Lindon, Utah.
qE-fBOjwIdY: Years earlier, an owner on the East Coast had to part with her Ludwig piano but couldn't bring herself to give up three pieces of it -- the front panel, the fall board, and a top lid decorated with cherub carvings -- which she kept as decor in her home. When she later discovered we had another Ludwig from the same era ready to be restored, she asked us to combine her salvaged pieces with it for a full rebuild. We revived the case in its deep original veneer, replated the original pedals, hand-refinished the cast iron plate -- a step most restorers skip -- and fitted new tuning pins, strings, hammers, and soundboard repairs, and this rebuilt Ludwig now resides in Paoli, Pennsylvania.
gVfZDqzGfYk: This 1957 Steinway upright received a complete rebuild, from new strings, tuning pins, hammers, dampers, shanks, and hammer butts to a fresh jet-black finish with the fine surface striations that have long marked genuine Steinway craftsmanship. New keys, bushings, and trap work rounded out the project, along with a humidity-control system to help protect the piano over time. Finished just before delivery, this piano is now ready to bring music to a family in Provo, Utah for generations to come.
62FB8Uwjtno: This 1936 Gulbransen was the childhood piano of a woman now approaching 90, and she chose to have it restored not for herself but as a keepsake for her grandchildren and great-grandchildren. We refinished the case, reviving veneer that had faded over decades in the sun, fitted new keys, and preserved its uncommon grand-piano-style lyre, a detail that would have been shaped entirely by hand in the 1930s, long before modern machinery existed. Inside, a full refurbishing reshaped and voiced the hammers and brought the action back into proper regulation, so this piano can keep making music for the family in Orem, Utah for years to come.
UCoG-TKU_PQ: This 1913 Mason & Hamlin, built during piano-making's golden age alongside Steinway and Baldwin, had sat partly disassembled in a family member's garage in California for years after an ambitious attempt at restoration stalled, before it eventually came to us for a complete rebuild. Along the way it had lost several original parts, including its entire lyre assembly, so we researched period-correct details and custom-built a replacement, while also returning the case to its original veneer after it had been coated over in black lacquer during that earlier, unfinished effort. New strings, hammers, dampers, and a fully rebuilt action bring this century-old grand back to its full voice in Provo, Utah.
Vf4KPGY4YFM: This 1890 Emerson upright arrived with cracking through its case finish and a pin block worn from decades of tension, so our shop saturated the surface damage until the wood could take no more glue and swapped in a freshly cut maple pin block where the wear ran deepest. We also refinished the soundboard, restored proper tension with hand-fit shims, repainted the cast plate, and brought its original 1890s pedals back to a like-new shine. The piano now has a rich, warm bass again in East Bend, North Carolina.
hqaUwcs65Kk: This antique upright runs on what piano technicians call a birdcage action, an unusual and finicky mechanism so intimidating that many technicians won't even attempt to work on one. We kept its carved case, original ivory keys, and hand-tied stringing intact while rebuilding the hammers and shanks, repairing a soundboard full of cracks with new tension and shims, and crafting a new bridge that matched the original string spacing exactly. The dampening now performs better than this old-style action was ever designed to, and the piano has found its new home in Las Vegas, Nevada.
GJ6MxpKwlGg: This turn-of-the-century Walworth Schulz upright received a complete rebuild, from a rich, dark refinish and freshly repainted plate to all-new strings, hammers, shanks, and dampers timed to lift in perfect unison. Every tuning pin was reset and torqued to factory-tight specifications, and the pedal trap work was rebuilt for smooth, silent operation. Restored to a warm, rich tone, this piano now belongs to a home in Morgan, Utah.
ixTdI5MNoW0: This 1931 Aeolian upright began life as a Duo-Art player piano, its pneumatic mechanism long since obsolete. We gave the case, keys, and action a full rebuild, new hammers, dampers, and strings throughout, and, rather than restoring the old pneumatics, fit a modern electronic player system discreetly inside the cabinet so it can still perform on its own. Standing nearly five feet tall with a big, room-filling tone, it now plays on for a family in Wenatchee, Washington.
iLvFiKDyBek: This 1907 Kimball upright was refinished in a natural, unstained lacquer that lets the wood's own pale color show through, and fitted with new keys and replated original hardware. Inside, we left a portion of the cast plate unpainted to preserve the manufacturer's original 1893 World's Columbian Exposition marking, a piece of the piano's own history, while restringing it and repairing the soundboard beneath. Fully restored, it now resides in South Jordan, Utah.
7QY_-6PpFIg: This 1907 American-built upright is typical of the pianos we restore most often, instruments that once signaled a family's rise into the middle class around the turn of the century. For its family, though, it means much more: it holds memories of a mother who played it for decades, accompanying her church, her community, and her own children whenever they wanted to sing. Rebuilt from the strings and hammers to the dampers and keys, this cherished family heirloom now plays again in Highland, Utah.
Nk-JcBppFyg: Built roughly a century ago in Chicago as a white-label piano for a Provo furniture and piano store, this upright needed a new upper bridge, which we redesigned in CAD and machined from scratch after the original had cracked throughout. We rebuilt the strings, hammers, dampers, and action, refinished the case and soundboard, and replated the original pedals and hardware rather than replacing them. Once owned by a close friend and mentor of our shop's founder, it now carries on with his granddaughter in Ankeny, Iowa.
Rv785PrgBDU: This 1905 Everett grand had been passed down through a family for three generations, played first by a mother and then by her sons, and by the time it reached our shop its case and mechanism reflected a century of steady use. We gave it a full rebuild, replacing the action from the shanks and hammers to the dampers, refinishing the soundboard, restoring tension to the board, and repainting the plate to a rich, even shine. Restored to full voice, it now carries on as a cherished family heirloom in Highland, Utah.
CD-YA2VElx0: This 1948 Baldwin R grand received a complete restoration, from a fresh set of strings, hammers, and dampers to an entirely rebuilt action. The plate was repainted to a rich, gleaming finish, the pedals and hardware were replated, and a faithful replica decal was added to complete the look. Technicians in the shop singled it out for its big, powerful tone, and it now plays on in Mapleton, Utah.
dYkY3JFTans: This 1911 Ludwig upright arrived at our shop with an already unusual case, hand-painted years earlier with an Asian-inspired mural the family wanted to preserve, so we left the artwork untouched. Inside told a different story: past water damage had rusted the strings, curled the hammers, and worn away the bridges, so we replaced the bridge caps, repaired the soundboard, and rebuilt the action from the ground up. What had been a piano too damaged to play now has a warm, rich tone again in Ogden, Utah.
bB0yozV6aAs: This compact 1977 Mason & Hamlin upright, one of the smaller models the storied maker produced in that era, received a full restoration: new strings, hammers, dampers, and action, a refinished soundboard, and a freshly painted plate. We also refinished the case in a rich satin black and added a silent-play system, so it can be practiced quietly through headphones as easily as it can fill a room with sound. It now brings both old craftsmanship and modern convenience to a home in Pasadena, California.
RaVQ3UZLCWE: This diminutive 1937 Wurlitzer upright, with its art deco lines and unusual cello-like sound openings built into the case, arrived in rough shape and needed a complete top-to-bottom restoration. We refinished the case, repainted the plate, and replaced the strings, hammers, dampers, shanks, and bushings throughout the action. Despite having fewer keys than a standard piano, it now produces a surprisingly full, rich tone in Morgan, Utah.
sMUe9VFLpc0: This 1892 Kimball upright arrived in the roughest condition we had seen in a long while, so thick with decades of grime and neglect inside that it could not be played at all. Rather than refinish the case, we spent hours carefully cleaning and preserving its original wood, then turned to the inside for a deep clean, new keys and bushings, reshaped hammers, and a full regulation to bring it back to life. New pedals and casters were added along the way, and this long-silenced piano now plays again in Manti, Utah.
c5NkmCJYWEs: This 1911 Wellington upright came to us with a heavily cracked, alligatored finish that we refinished to a smooth, rich shine, along with a faithful replica of its original decal. Inside, we gave it new tuning pins, strings, hammers, and dampers, repaired the soundboard, and rebuilt the bridges, while new keytops and replated pedals rounded out the work. Ready for another century of use, it now lives in Millcreek, Utah.
fNgg4gjBYC4: This quirky Marxophone — a fretless novelty instrument once sold door-to-door around the turn of the century for people who couldn't afford a full piano — arrived at our workshop with a cracked case. At the owner's request we repaired the crack and touched it up with lacquer while leaving its well-worn character intact, then fitted new hand-built bass strings, harpsichord-style tuning pins, and fresh felt to bring its odd little voice back to life. It's now back home in San Francisco, California, ready for its next round of musical experiments.
QYwxv-g8oiI: By 1884 standards this Steinway upright had led a hard life, and although its case had been refinished in some earlier decade, the piano's mechanical heart — strings, hammers, dampers, shanks, and felt — was still all original and thoroughly worn out. We rebuilt the entire action from scratch, repolished the gilded plate, resurfaced the original ivory keys rather than replacing them, and replated the pedals and hardware. The piano now plays and sings again in Dana Point, California.
ATzMaea_MGQ: When a house fire broke out in this family's kitchen, the smoke and heat reached the living room and left this 1967 Everett upright coated in soot, leaving pale outlines on its lid where photos and keepsakes had once rested, imprinted by the heat around them. After several days in an ozone chamber to lift the smoke smell for good, we refinished the case to a shine better than it likely had in decades and polished up its original brass pedals and hardware, along with a full restoration of the instrument inside. Today it's back making music, smoke-free, for a grateful family in Pleasant Grove, Utah.
igvsDgbYmnY: This 1903 Everett upright came out of its rebuild looking almost unrecognizable, with a rich satin ebony finish that holds up even in direct sunlight and gleaming polished pedals and hardware. Inside, we refinished the plate, polished the original mounting bolts, cleaned and selectively replaced the antique ivory keytops, and rebuilt the action with new hammers and dampers, including upgraded triple dampers in the bass for quieter, more controlled tone. More than a century after it was built, this piano — proof that a well-restored antique can outplay a modern instrument — is heading home to a family in Lake Elmo, Minnesota.
ZJ8EMufEtGw: This 1966 Wurlitzer upright belonged to a client since childhood, and at his request we kept the original keys exactly as they were, chips, cracks, and all, because those were the keys he learned to play on before going on to perform professionally in a classic rock band. We refinished the case, repaired and re-tensioned the soundboard with new shims, and rebuilt the action from the ground up with new hammers, dampers, strings, tuning pins, and hardware. The piano that shaped a musician's childhood now sounds better than ever back home in Pleasant Grove, Utah.
xOn5soIdpZw: This 1968 Yamaha upright had been passed from a piano teacher to a hardworking professional musician, and years of heavy playing had left its action in truly rough shape, with a badly out-of-regulation touch and a set of worn, discolored keys. Working within a practical budget, we focused on function over cosmetics: refinishing the keys, replacing broken action cords common to Yamahas of this era, and fully correcting the let-off, dip, and blow regulation so the piano would actually play the way it should, while leaving the still-serviceable strings and dampers in place. The piano plays like new again for its owner in Spanish Fork, Utah.
XDmBRrmrvt4: With its elaborately carved legs, the work of specialty piano-leg makers from a bygone era, this 1890 Camp & Sons upright had been in one family for generations, though decades of use had left its action essentially unplayable and its soundboard and bridges badly cracked. We gave it a complete rebuild: a fresh plate finish, new bridge caps, new strings, tuning pins, hammers, and dampers, along with a from-scratch refinish of its originally black case and fresh plating on the pedals and hardware, all while preserving the family's original ivory keys at their request. This cherished heirloom now plays beautifully again in Pleasant Grove, Utah.
